input.content:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
The local turn-based grid engine under `/app` produces resume audits where opposing sides step through blocked tiles or act from positions the human cannot currently see, but uninterrupted runs stay clean. Running `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` writes `/app/output/resume-audit.json`. Bundled layout maps under `/app/data`, action scripts under `/app/data/scripts`, and tables under `/app/config` define the inputs each case name uses; a correct audit reconciles both execution paths against those inputs.

The audit JSON has a top-level cases array for rook, bishop, knight, lancer, sentinel, and warden. Each case uses name, ok, fresh, resume, moves, visibility, effects, records, and log. The fresh and resume summaries describe the same scenario through the two execution paths and carry illegal, steps, and sig. The sig value is the engine signature over the resolved move list for that path, not a new per-run identifier. Each move entry carries turn, side id, from coordinates, to coordinates, kind, and legal.

Records points to a JSONL file whose nonblank rows parse to the same list in the same order. Log points to whitespace-delimited rows in the same order. The audit is clean when every case has ok true, both summaries report illegal 0, signatures match when both paths ran opposing turns, and the JSON, records file, and log file all describe the same resolved rows.

The matrix run also leaves `/app/bin/rogctl` executable. Running `bash /app/scripts/one-case.sh <case>` writes `/app/output/single-<case>.json` with the same top-level cases wrapper and the one requested row, matching that row from the matrix audit.


Current terminal state:
Current Terminal Screen:
root@f8bd583533f9:/app#

{
  "analysis": "We are at /app with no prior commands run in this session. Need to reproduce the resume-audit.json by running the matrix script, then inspect failures: cases where ok is false, illegal > 0, signatures mismatch, or records/log disagree. Then identify engine/input mismatch causing resume path to allow unseen/blocked actions, implement fix in code/config, and re-run matrix and per-case checks until all cases are clean and one-case output matches matrix row.",
  "plan": "1) Run the matrix audit to generate /app/output/resume-audit.json and associated records/log files. 2) Summarize which cases fail and why by extracting ok/illegal/sig and file pointers. 3) For a failing case, open its referenced records JSONL and log to confirm divergence, then run one-case to reproduce in isolation. 4) Locate relevant engine code and inputs for that case (map/script/tables), then adjust engine resume reconciliation so both paths resolve identical legal moves (especially visibility/blocked-tile checks) and produce stable signatures. 5) Re-run matrix and verify all cases ok and file consistency, plus spot-check one-case matches matrix.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh
",
      "duration": 10.0
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la /app/output
",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "python3 - <<'PY'
import json
p='/app/output/resume-audit.json'
print('exists',p)
with open(p) as f: data=json.load(f)
print('cases',len(data.get('cases',[])))
for c in data.get('cases',[]):
    name=c.get('name')
    ok=c.get('ok')
    fresh=c.get('fresh',{})
    resume=c.get('resume',{})
    print(f"{name:9s} ok={ok} fresh(illegal={fresh.get('illegal')}, steps={fresh.get('steps')}, sig={fresh.get('sig')}) resume(illegal={resume.get('illegal')}, steps={resume.get('steps')}, sig={resume.get('sig')}) records={c.get('records')} log={c.get('log')}")
PY
",
      "duration": 1.0
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "python3 - <<'PY'
import json
p='/app/output/resume-audit.json'
with open(p) as f: data=json.load(f)
bad=[]
for c in data['cases']:
    if not c.get('ok') or c.get('fresh',{}).get('illegal') or c.get('resume',{}).get('illegal'):
        bad.append(c['name'])
print('bad',bad)
PY
",
      "duration": 1.0
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": false
}

{
  "analysis": "Matrix audit generated successfully. Two cases fail: rook and sentinel. Both have illegal=0 and steps=1 in fresh and resume, but fresh.sig != resume.sig, so the resolved move list differs between fresh and resume for those cases. Need to inspect each case’s moves plus the referenced records.jsonl and trace.tsv, compare fresh vs resume move rows, then reproduce with one-case to isolate and trace where resume path diverges.",
  "plan": "1) Dump rook and sentinel entries (moves/visibility/effects/records/log) from resume-audit.json to see exactly what differs. 2) Parse and print the records.jsonl and trace.tsv for rook/sentinel and compare them to the audit moves order/content. 3) Run one-case.sh rook and sentinel and diff their single-case output against the matrix row to ensure reproduction. 4) Once we see the exact move differences (from/to/side/turn/kind/legal), we’ll trace to engine code that computes visibility / blockage / legal moves during resume and fix the discrepancy.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "python3 - <<'PY'
import json, pprint
p='/app/output/resume-audit.json'
with open(p) as f: data=json.load(f)
for name in ['rook','sentinel']:
    c=next(x for x in data['cases'] if x['name']==name)
    print('\n===',name,'===')
    for k in ['ok','fresh','resume','records','log']:
        print(k, c.get(k))
    print('moves:')
    pprint.pp(c.get('moves'))
    print('visibility:')
    pprint.pp(c.get('visibility'))
    print('effects:')
    pprint.pp(c.get('effects'))
    print('records_meta:')
    pprint.pp(c.get('records_meta'))
    print('log_meta:')
    pprint.pp(c.get('log_meta'))
PY
",
      "duration": 1.0
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "for c in rook sentinel; do echo "\n## $c records"; nl -ba /app/workspace/$c/records.jsonl | sed -n '1,200p'; done
",
      "duration": 1.0
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "for c in rook sentinel; do echo "\n## $c trace"; nl -ba /app/workspace/$c/trace.tsv | sed -n '1,200p'; done
",
      "duration": 1.0
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "bash /app/scripts/one-case.sh rook
",
      "duration": 2.0
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "bash /app/scripts/one-case.sh sentinel
",
      "duration": 2.0
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "python3 - <<'PY'
import json

def load_case(path):
    with open(path) as f: d=json.load(f)
    return d['cases'][0]

m='/app/output/resume-audit.json'
with open(m) as f: md=json.load(f)
for name in ['rook','sentinel']:
    mc=next(x for x in md['cases'] if x['name']==name)
    sc=load_case(f'/app/output/single-{name}.json')
    print('\n== compare',name,'matrix vs single ==')
    for k in ['ok','fresh','resume','moves','visibility','effects']:
        same = (mc.get(k)==sc.get(k))
        print(k,'same' if same else 'DIFF')
PY
",
      "duration": 1.0
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": false
}

import json

def load_case(path):
    with open(path) as f: d=json.load(f)
    return d['cases'][0]

m='/app/output/resume-audit.json'
with open(m) as f: md=json.load(f)
for name in ['rook','sentinel']:
    mc=next(x for x in md['cases'] if x['name']==name)
    sc=load_case(f'/app/output/single-{name}.json')
    print('\n== compare',name,'matrix vs single ==')
    for k in ['ok','fresh','resume','moves','visibility','effects']:
        same = (mc.get(k)==sc.get(k))
        print(k,'same' if same else 'DIFF')
PY

You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
The local turn-based grid engine under `/app` produces resume audits where opposing sides step through blocked tiles or act from positions the human cannot currently see, but uninterrupted runs stay clean. Running `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` writes `/app/output/resume-audit.json`. Bundled layout maps under `/app/data`, action scripts under `/app/data/scripts`, and tables under `/app/config` define the inputs each case name uses; a correct audit reconciles both execution paths against those inputs.

The audit JSON has a top-level cases array for rook, bishop, knight, lancer, sentinel, and warden. Each case uses name, ok, fresh, resume, moves, visibility, effects, records, and log. The fresh and resume summaries describe the same scenario through the two execution paths and carry illegal, steps, and sig. The sig value is the engine signature over the resolved move list for that path, not a new per-run identifier. Each move entry carries turn, side id, from coordinates, to coordinates, kind, and legal.

Records points to a JSONL file whose nonblank rows parse to the same list in the same order. Log points to whitespace-delimited rows in the same order. The audit is clean when every case has ok true, both summaries report illegal 0, signatures match when both paths ran opposing turns, and the JSON, records file, and log file all describe the same resolved rows.

The matrix run also leaves `/app/bin/rogctl` executable. Running `bash /app/scripts/one-case.sh <case>` writes `/app/output/single-<case>.json` with the same top-level cases wrapper and the one requested row, matching that row from the matrix audit.

> import json
>
> def load_case(path):
